# **A Comprehensive Playbook for Gravitational Microlensing Data Visualization and Plotting Guidelines**

The analysis of gravitational microlensing events represents a unique intersection of observational astrophysics, complex mathematical modeling, and high-dimensional statistical inference. Unlike transits or radial velocity measurements, microlensing relies on the transient, unrepeatable magnification of a background source star caused by the gravitational potential of an intervening lens system passing across the line of sight.1 Because this phenomenon typically occurs on galactic scales—where the multiple distorted images of the background source cannot be individually resolved by current optical telescopes—researchers must infer the physical properties of the lens system entirely through indirect observables.1 These observables include time-series photometric lightcurves, subtle astrometric centroid shifts, and sophisticated multidimensional parameter modeling.1

With the impending data deluge from next-generation observatories such as the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope, the Vera C. Rubin Observatory, and the Euclid mission, the volume of detected microlensing events will increase by orders of magnitude.5 The Roman Space Telescope alone is projected to identify analogs to nearly every planet in our solar system, probing cold, wide-orbit exoplanets down to sub-Earth masses.5 To manage this exponential growth in data and to facilitate collaboration across the global astronomical community, standardizing the visual grammar of microlensing analysis is of paramount importance. Robust plotting conventions are critical for breaking mathematical degeneracies, isolating subtle planetary anomalies from systematic noise, and establishing uniform Bayesian inference frameworks for determining physical parameters.7

This comprehensive playbook provides an exhaustive, expert-level detailing of the plotting guidelines, mathematical foundations, and standard aesthetic conventions required for visualizing microlensing data. It encompasses the full spectrum of required visualizations: from fundamental photometric lightcurves and residual diagnostics, to geometric caustic topologies, astrometric trajectories, multidimensional likelihood maps, banana plots for degeneracy resolution, and ultimate physical parameter inferences.

## **1\. Photometric Time-Series Visualization: Lightcurves, Anomalies, and Residual Analysis**

The foundational visualization in any microlensing study is the photometric lightcurve. This plot tracks the apparent brightening and subsequent fading of a background source star as the gravitational lens traverses the line of sight.1 In the most basic physical scenario—a single source lensed by a single point mass, known as the point-source point-lens (PSPL) model—the observed magnification is purely a function of the normalized projected separation between the lens and the source.9

### **1.1 Theoretical Foundations of the Point-Source Point-Lens Model**

To properly construct and interpret a microlensing lightcurve, one must understand the underlying magnification function. The theoretical magnification ![][image1] of a PSPL event is defined by the equation:

![][image2]  
In this equation, ![][image3] represents the angular separation between the lens and the source star, normalized by the angular Einstein radius (![][image4]) of the lens.9 As ![][image3] approaches zero—meaning the lens and source are in near-perfect alignment from the observer's perspective—the theoretical magnification diverges toward infinity, creating the characteristic symmetric, bell-shaped curve observed in standard microlensing events.9 For events where the lens hosts a planetary companion, the secondary mass acts as a perturbation, creating localized anomalies or spikes superimposed on this baseline PSPL curve.1

### **1.2 Aesthetic and Scaling Conventions for Multi-Observatory Lightcurves**

The accurate plotting of microlensing lightcurves requires strict adherence to specific axes conventions and scaling rules to ensure that data from disparate global observatories can be compared seamlessly. Standard microlensing lightcurves plot the temporal progression of the event on the horizontal x-axis and the observed brightness on the vertical y-axis.11

The time axis is universally represented using Heliocentric Julian Date (HJD) or Barycentric Julian Date (BJD). Because raw Julian dates are unwieldy seven-digit numbers, standard plotting practice dictates subtracting a constant integer offset from the time array to improve readability.13 A common offset is 2,450,000 days, resulting in an x-axis label denoted as HJD \- 2450000\.13 This offset must be explicitly stated in the axis label to prevent chronological ambiguity.

The vertical photometric axis is plotted using either apparent magnitude or absolute magnification/flux. When apparent magnitude is utilized, standard astronomical convention dictates that the y-axis must be inverted, placing numerically smaller values (which correspond to brighter apparent magnitudes) at the top of the plot.13 This inversion ensures that the visual representation aligns with the physical reality of the source star brightening. When absolute magnification or raw flux is plotted, a linear scale is typically employed.13 However, for high-magnification events—such as KMT-2022-BLG-0440, where the peak magnification ![][image5] exceeded 65—the y-axis should be transitioned to a logarithmic scale near the peak to prevent the extreme central spike from compressing the baseline data points into illegibility.14

Because modern microlensing campaigns rely on continuous, round-the-clock monitoring from networks of geographically distributed telescopes, a single lightcurve plot will often incorporate data from multiple observatories, such as the Optical Gravitational Lensing Experiment (OGLE), the Microlensing Observations in Astrophysics (MOA), the Korea Microlensing Telescope Network (KMTNet), and the Las Cumbres Observatory Global Telescope (LCOGT) network.11 Data points from different observatories must be strictly differentiated using highly distinct color palettes and geometric marker styles.11 For example, OGLE data might be represented by blue circles, KMTNet data by red squares, and MOA data by green triangles.16 Because ground-based surveys operate in highly crowded fields with differing degrees of atmospheric seeing and instrumental blending, the baseline unmagnified flux measured by each telescope will vary. Therefore, prior to plotting, the modeling software must align and scale the flux of all datasets to match the baseline flux established by one primary reference dataset, typically the one with the highest photometric precision or the longest baseline.13

The theoretical model—whether a simple 1L1S (single-lens, single-source) PSPL model or a complex 2L1S (binary-lens, single-source) planetary model—is overlaid onto the scatter plot of observational data as a continuous line.11 In complex modeling scenarios where multiple models are being compared, the global best-fit 2L1S model is traditionally plotted as a thick, solid black line.11 To visually emphasize the deviation caused by the planetary companion, the baseline 1L1S model is simultaneously plotted as a dashed or dotted line.11 For events that exhibit pronounced finite-source effects—occurring when the angular radius of the source star (![][image6]) is comparable to or larger than the impact parameter—the theoretical model curve must reflect the convolution of the finite stellar disk's surface brightness profile with the complex magnification map, which acts to truncate and smooth the theoretical infinite peaks of the underlying point-source model.17

### **1.3 Residual Panels, Correlated Noise, and Gaussian Process Overlays**

A primary lightcurve plot is considered analytically incomplete without the inclusion of a corresponding residuals panel. This panel is plotted immediately beneath the primary lightcurve, sharing the identical horizontal time axis.11 The residuals panel provides a critical visual diagnostic of the model's goodness-of-fit and is the primary tool for identifying low-amplitude planetary deviations that might be invisible to the naked eye on the main plot.

The vertical axis of the residuals panel is scaled in one of two ways: either in absolute magnitudes (![][image7]) representing the difference between the observed data and the theoretical model, or in standard deviations (![][image8]) representing the statistical significance of the deviation.11 To mitigate visual clutter and overplotting in data-dense surveys that sample events multiple times per hour, the data points in the residual plot are frequently binned into discrete temporal windows, with error bars representing the standard error of the binned mean.21

In the densely crowded stellar fields of the Galactic Bulge, traditional independent, identically distributed (white) noise models are often insufficient to capture the true photometric uncertainty. Systematics such as seeing variations, weather patterns, and blending from unresolved neighbor stars introduce highly correlated (red) noise into the photometric measurements.16 It is considered best practice in modern microlensing visualization to model this correlated noise using Gaussian Processes (GPs).16 The predictive mean and variance of the GP model should be overlaid directly onto the residual panel as a continuous shaded band or a solid line.16 This visual inclusion of the GP prevents the viewer from misinterpreting a systematic baseline drift or correlated noise artifact as a physical planetary anomaly.

Finally, because planetary anomalies, caustic crossings, and short-lived binary source deviations often persist for only a few hours to a few days within an event that may last for months, a comprehensive lightcurve visualization must feature one or more zoomed-in inset panels.11 These insets should be tightly centered on the time of the closest approach (![][image9]) or the specific epoch of the anomaly, allowing the viewer to inspect the exact structure of the deviation, the cadence of the observations capturing it, and the fidelity of the model's fit to the peak magnification.11

| Visual Component | Standard Plotting Convention | Analytical Purpose |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Primary X-Axis** | HJD \- 2450000 | Standardizes chronological reporting; removes cumbersome leading digits. |
| **Primary Y-Axis** | Apparent Magnitude (Inverted) or Linear/Log Magnification | Visualizes the transient brightening; inversion aligns with standard astronomical magnitude scales. |
| **Residuals Y-Axis** | **![][image7]** or Standard Deviations (![][image8]) | Highlights localized deviations from the baseline theoretical model. |
| **Model Lines** | Solid curve (Best-fit 2L1S), Dashed curve (1L1S baseline) | Differentiates complex multi-body models from simple point-lens models, emphasizing the planetary signal. |
| **Observational Data** | Distinct colors and marker geometries per observatory | Accounts for varying baseline fluxes, survey cadences, and instrumental systematics across distinct global telescopes. |
| **Noise Modeling** | Gaussian Process (GP) bands overlaid on residuals | Differentiates true physical anomalies from correlated red noise and atmospheric systematics. |

## **2\. Geometric and Topological Mapping: Caustic Diagrams and Magnification Heatmaps**

When a gravitational lens system consists of two or more masses—such as a host star orbited by an exoplanet, or a binary star system—the gravitational potential becomes highly complex and asymmetric. The solutions to the lens equation in these multi-body scenarios result in continuous, closed boundaries in the source plane known as caustics.9 At these exact theoretical boundaries, the magnification of a point source diverges to infinity.9 Visualizing the structural topology of these caustics, and mapping the trajectory of the background source star across them, is absolutely paramount for understanding the physical geometry driving the observed photometric lightcurve.

### **2.1 The Mathematics of Caustic Formation and Lens Equation Solutions**

In binary-lens microlensing, the behavior of the system is fundamentally governed by two primary geometric parameters: the mass ratio of the secondary body to the primary body (![][image10]), and the projected two-dimensional separation between the two bodies normalized by the angular Einstein radius (![][image11]).23 Depending on the specific values of ![][image11] and ![][image10], the lens equation generates caustics of varying shapes and sizes. Caustics are defined as mathematical lines of zero physical width.9 They are geometrically composed of smooth, concave, inward-curving segments known as folds, which intersect at sharp, highly localized points of extreme magnification known as cusps.9

Binary lens systems exhibit three distinct topological regimes based on the projected separation ![][image11]. A "close" binary (![][image12]) produces a central caustic near the primary star and two small, offset planetary caustics.24 A "wide" binary (![][image13]) produces a single central caustic and one distant planetary caustic.24 An "intermediate" or resonant binary (![][image14]) produces a single, large, continuous resonant caustic structure that connects the central and planetary regions, typically resulting in events with incredibly high peak magnifications and complex, multi-peaked lightcurves.14

### **2.2 Visualizing Magnification Gradients and Topological Boundaries**

A standard caustic diagram plots the ![][image15] source plane, normalized by the angular Einstein radius ![][image4].9 By convention, the primary lens mass (the host star) is placed at the geometric origin ![][image16], or the entire coordinate system is centered on the center of mass of the binary lens system.24 The caustic curves themselves are plotted as solid, brightly colored lines—frequently red or blue—to ensure they stand out sharply against the background grid.9

To provide deeper physical intuition, the region surrounding the caustic curves is often shaded using a continuous, highly detailed color map to represent the magnification gradient across the source plane.9 Brighter colors or warmer hues indicate regions of higher magnification, while darker colors represent lower magnification.9 A fundamental rule of microlensing topology visually represented in these maps is that the magnification inside any closed caustic curve is universally greater than the magnification immediately outside of it.9

In situations where multiple degenerate models fit the photometric data equally well—such as the infamous close/wide degeneracy where a system with separation ![][image11] produces a nearly identical caustic perturbation to a system with separation ![][image17]—it is standard practice to plot the caustic diagrams of the competing models in adjacent, side-by-side panels.11 This side-by-side presentation clearly demonstrates to the reader how vastly different physical geometries can produce almost indistinguishable photometric lightcurves.11

### **2.3 Source Trajectory Mapping and Finite-Source Effect Visualization**

A caustic diagram is a static representation of the lens geometry; to introduce the element of time and connect the geometry to the observed lightcurve, the trajectory of the background source star must be plotted across the caustic map.24

The source trajectory is typically plotted as a straight line transversing the magnification map, though it may be depicted as a slightly curved or undulating path if microlens parallax (caused by the Earth's orbital motion) is a significant factor in the event.16 The geometric path is uniquely defined by two parameters: the impact parameter ![][image18] (the minimum separation between the source and the reference center of the lens system) and the angle ![][image19] representing the trajectory's angle relative to the binary axis of symmetry.23 To directly correlate the spatial caustic diagram with the temporal photometric lightcurve, the trajectory line is annotated with evenly spaced geometric dots, tick marks, or crosshairs representing specific chronological intervals, such as 10-day or 100-day increments.19

Crucially, the background source star is not a dimensionless point. When visualizing high-magnification events or events with direct caustic crossings, the finite size of the source star must be explicitly represented.17 The source is visualized on the trajectory line as an open or semi-transparent colored circle, mathematically scaled to the normalized source radius ![][image20].17 The visual representation of this finite disk is vital for communicating the physical reality of a caustic crossing: as the leading edge of the finite source disk crosses the zero-width caustic line, the theoretical infinite magnification is integrated and smoothed over the surface area of the star, resulting in the finite, measurable peaks observed in the photometric data.11

## **3\. Navigating Parameter Space Topography: Grid Search Likelihood and ![][image21] Maps**

The mathematical modeling of binary and multi-body microlensing events represents a notoriously difficult computational challenge. The parameter space is highly non-linear, aggressively chaotic, and fraught with severe degeneracies and deep local minima.11 Standard gradient-descent optimization algorithms will almost inevitably become trapped in local minima, resulting in entirely incorrect physical interpretations. To ensure that the true global minimum is identified, researchers must conduct exhaustive, computationally intensive grid searches over the most non-linear parameters—specifically the mass ratio ![][image10] and the projected separation ![][image11].23 The visualization of this massive computational effort is the ![][image21] grid search likelihood map.

### **3.1 Constructing the ![][image22] Parameter Grid**

The grid search map provides a topographical overview of the model's goodness-of-fit across the entirety of plausible physical geometries. The horizontal x-axis of the map represents the base-10 logarithm of the normalized projected separation, ![][image23], while the vertical y-axis represents the base-10 logarithm of the mass ratio, ![][image24].23

To construct the map, the ![][image22] parameter space is divided into a discrete, high-resolution grid. Standard methodical spacing for this grid is typically on the order of ![][image25] increments in ![][image23] and ![][image26] increments in ![][image24], though finer resolutions are often employed depending on available computational resources.23 At each individual node on this grid, the ![][image22] values are held fixed, while a genetic algorithm or an initial Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm is deployed to optimize all other linear and non-linear parameters (such as ![][image27]).23 The underlying grid points evaluated during this process may be represented visually on the final map using small geometric symbols, such as red crosses or faint dots, to convey the resolution and exhaustiveness of the search.23

### **3.2 Visualizing Likelihood Topography and Contour Levels**

Once the lowest ![][image28] value for every node on the grid is calculated, the relative goodness-of-fit is mapped as a continuous topographical surface. This is achieved by plotting contour lines representing specific ![][image21] values relative to the single global best-fitting model found during the search.23 Standard intervals for plotting these contour lines are ![][image29] and ![][image30].23

The regions bounded by these contour lines are filled with a continuous color gradient to provide immediate visual intuition. Typically, a sequential colormap utilizing shades of blue, purple, or a viridis scale is employed, where the darkest, most intense colors visually denote the deepest ![][image28] minima (the most probable physical configurations), fading to white or light gray in regions of poor fit.23

### **3.3 Visualizing the Close/Wide Degeneracy and Topological Boundaries**

The primary scientific utility of the ![][image21] grid search map is its unparalleled ability to visually communicate the presence of the close/wide degeneracy.14 Because an event with a planetary separation ![][image11] produces a nearly identical central caustic perturbation to an event with a separation ![][image17], a properly constructed grid search map will almost universally feature two distinct, dark "islands" of high probability.23 These two minima islands will be located symmetrically across the ![][image31] (![][image32]) vertical axis.23

To further aid in the physical interpretation of the likelihood map, the mathematical boundaries separating the close, intermediate (resonant), and wide topological regimes are often demarcated across the parameter space using dashed overlay curves.23 Additionally, physical constraints derived directly from the lightcurve—such as contours of constant Einstein crossing time (![][image33])—may be superimposed on the ![][image22] plane as distinct, highly visible lines (e.g., solid orange curves), illustrating how timing constraints slice through the geometric probability space.23 Once the distinct minima are identified visually via the grid search map, researchers select the parameters at the absolute bottom of these minima to seed the final, high-resolution MCMC chains for exact parameter estimation and uncertainty quantification.23

## **4\. Bayesian Inference Visualization: Corner Plots and High-Dimensional Posteriors**

Because the traditional observables derived from a microlensing lightcurve (such as the event timescale ![][image33], the time of maximum magnification ![][image9], and the impact parameter ![][image18]) represent complex, interdependent combinations of the underlying physical parameters of the system (lens mass, lens distance, source distance, and relative transverse velocity), exact parameter estimation relies entirely on Bayesian inference.8 Researchers utilize MCMC sampling algorithms to comprehensively map the posterior probability distributions of the parameter space.26 The results of these high-dimensional samplings are visualized using corner plots, also widely known within the astronomical community as triangle plots.29

### **4.1 The Anatomy of a Microlensing Corner Plot**

A corner plot is a dense, lower-triangular grid of subplots designed to simultaneously visualize the marginalized one-dimensional probabilities of individual parameters and the two-dimensional covariances between every possible pair of parameters.25

The diagonal line of panels cascading from the top-left to the bottom-right of the grid displays the marginalized one-dimensional posterior Probability Density Function (PDF) for each individual model parameter.25 These are plotted as step-filled histograms or smoothed kernel density estimates.25 Standard parameters plotted along this diagonal include the geometric lightcurve parameters (![][image34]), the finite source ratio (![][image35]), the binary lens parameters (![][image36]), and the components of the microlens parallax vector (![][image37]).25 Vertical lines drawn across these 1D histograms denote the median value of the distribution, as well as the ![][image38] (![][image39] and ![][image40] percentile) credible intervals.30

The off-diagonal panels, which make up the bulk of the triangle, display the two-dimensional joint posterior distributions for every possible pairing of the parameters. These panels are visualized as 2D histograms, scatter plots of the MCMC chain samples, or, most commonly, as contour plots enclosing the ![][image38], ![][image41], and ![][image42] probability volumes.25 These 2D panels are crucial for visually highlighting the covariances and complex degeneracies that exist between parameters.25

Axis labeling on corner plots must adhere strictly to standard LaTeX formatting conventions to accurately represent mathematical variables (e.g., ![][image33] \[days\], ![][image18], ![][image24]). In advanced scenarios involving multisignal models—such as the millilensing of gravitational waves where a single source produces multiple discrete signals—the parameters representing the effective luminosity distance (![][image43]) and the time delays between images are explicitly indexed using a time-ordering convention (e.g., ![][image44]).30

### **4.2 Diagnostic Applications and Degeneracy Identification**

The corner plot serves as the definitive visual diagnostic test for evaluating the health, convergence, and physical validity of an MCMC run. Highly correlated parameters will manifest in the off-diagonal 2D panels as tightly stretched, diagonal ellipses rather than circular probability clouds.25 For example, the blending flux ratio ![][image45] (the ratio of lens flux to source flux) and the impact parameter ![][image18] frequently exhibit strong covariance, which is immediately visually apparent as a diagonal smear in their shared 2D panel.4

Furthermore, bimodal distributions in the 1D histograms, or dual, separated clusters in the 2D contour plots, indicate the presence of unresolved discrete degeneracies.19 A classic example is the ecliptic degeneracy, where the posterior distribution splits cleanly between a solution where ![][image46] and an almost identical solution where ![][image47].22

### **4.3 Advanced Applications: Gravitational Wave Millilensing and Machine Learning**

The utility of the corner plot extends beyond traditional photometric exoplanet detection. In the burgeoning field of gravitational wave millilensing—where gravitational waves emitted by binary black hole mergers are lensed by intermediate-mass objects—researchers utilize Bayesian inference libraries such as Bilby to perform parameter estimation.30 In the analysis of events like GW200208, corner plots of the millilensing parameters (effective luminosity distances and time delays) are used to determine if secondary signals are true lensed images or merely artifacts of instrumental noise; a true signal produces a sharp, localized peak in the 1D posterior, while noise results in a flat or extended underlying posterior.30

To address the immense computational cost of traditional Bayesian inference, researchers are increasingly injecting Conditional Variational Autoencoders (CVAEs) into the parameter estimation pipeline.28 When plotting the results of these machine learning-accelerated inferences, the corner plot generated by the CVAE is directly overlaid on top of the traditional Bilby corner plot.32 This comparative visualization is designed to definitively prove that the deep learning model achieves identical posterior contour shapes and parameter accuracy, while operating up to five orders of magnitude faster than standard MCMC samplers.28

| Corner Plot Component | Visual Representation | Diagnostic Function |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Diagonal Panels** | 1D Histograms / Density plots | Displays the marginalized probability and ![][image38] credible intervals for individual parameters. |
| **Off-Diagonal Panels** | 2D Contour maps / Scatter plots | Reveals parameter covariance, correlations, and continuous degeneracies (e.g., elongated ellipses). |
| **Bimodal Contours** | Separated probability clusters | Indicates unresolved discrete degeneracies (e.g., the ecliptic ![][image18] degeneracy). |
| **Overlaid Posteriors** | Overlapping, differently colored contours | Compares the accuracy of competing sampling algorithms (e.g., Bilby MCMC vs. CVAE). |

## **5\. Breaking Degeneracies: Banana Plots and the Intersection of Parallax and Lens Flux**

While the geometric parameters of the lens system (![][image48]) can often be extracted from the photometric lightcurve with high precision, deriving the actual physical mass (![][image49]) and distance (![][image50]) of the lens star requires breaking the fundamental microlensing degeneracy.8 A single measurement of the Einstein crossing time ![][image33] is insufficient, as it depends on the mass, distance, and relative velocity of the system. To isolate the physical parameters, the standard observables must be constrained by higher-order effects. The two most powerful constraints are the microlens parallax (![][image51]) and the measurement of intrinsic lens flux.34 The visualization of these intersecting constraints is commonly achieved through the use of "banana plots".25

### **5.1 The Microlens Parallax Constraint and Lightcurve Asymmetry**

Microlens parallax (![][image51]) arises from the orbital motion of the Earth around the Sun during the course of the microlensing event.10 Because the Earth is not a stationary observing platform, our perspective on the lens-source alignment shifts continuously.10 This relative change in perspective induces a subtle, asymmetric "wiggle" or deviation in the lightcurve relative to the perfect symmetry of a standard PSPL model.10

The microlensing parallax is a dimensionless vector quantity defined as the relative parallax of the lens and source (![][image52]) scaled by the angular Einstein radius: ![][image53].10 The component of the relative proper motion parallel to the ecliptic plane manifests as a distinct asymmetry in the lightcurve about the time of maximum brightness, while the perpendicular component causes a symmetric broadening or narrowing.16 To visualize the presence of parallax, researchers plot the residuals of a standard PSPL model against the residuals of a parallax-inclusive model; the divergence between the two residual traces visually confirms the parallax signal, which is most prominent in events with long timescales (large ![][image33]).10 In events with high-cadence, multi-observatory coverage—such as OGLE-2016-BLG-0156, which exhibited three distinct, widely separated peaks—the multiple peaks provide a very tight constraint on the microlens parallax effect, allowing for highly precise measurements.36

### **5.2 The Geometric Origin of the Banana Plot**

When microlens parallax (![][image51]) and finite-source effects (![][image4]) are both successfully measured from the lightcurve, they allow for the direct, algebraic calculation of the lens mass via the relation:

![][image54]  
However, these measurements—particularly the parallax vector—are frequently burdened by large, non-Gaussian uncertainties and degeneracies. When these uncertainties are propagated through the non-linear algebraic relationships and plotted in a 2D physical parameter space (such as mass vs. distance), the resulting credible intervals do not form neat circles or ellipses.25 Instead, the probability contours stretch and curve into elongated, arc-like swaths, colloquially known as "banana plots".25 These banana-shaped probability clouds visually represent the continuum of mass and distance combinations that are mathematically permitted by the lightcurve data.25

### **5.3 Intersecting Constraints via High-Resolution Adaptive Optics**

Because the banana-shaped contours derived solely from the lightcurve often permit a frustratingly wide range of physical masses, an independent, intersecting constraint is required. This is achieved by directly measuring the intrinsic flux of the lens star using high-resolution Adaptive Optics (AO) from 8-meter class ground telescopes (like Keck) or space-based platforms (like the Hubble Space Telescope).8

Because a star's mass is physically correlated with its luminosity, establishing a mass-luminosity relation ![][image55] in a specific observational passband (e.g., the ![][image56]\-band) places a strict independent constraint on the allowed combinations of ![][image57] and ![][image58].35 The apparent magnitude of the lens is calculated considering the distance modulus and the line-of-sight extinction, utilizing 3D dust maps.35

When plotting the final constraints, the banana-shaped contours derived from the photometric lightcurve (![][image59]) are overlaid on the same axes as the linear or orthogonal probability constraints derived from the high-resolution lens flux measurements.25 The precise geometric intersection of these distinct probability regions visually isolates the true physical parameters of the lens system, breaking the microlensing degeneracy entirely.25 This technique has been highly successful; for example, applying Bayesian flux constraints to events like MOA-2008-BLG-310 and OGLE-2012-BLG-0950 produced mass-distance predictions that were later perfectly validated by Keck and Hubble observations that directly resolved the lens and source separating years after the event.35

## **6\. Physical Parameter Inference: The Mass-Distance Plane**

The ultimate objective of any planetary microlensing campaign is the rigorous determination of the physical properties of the host star and its planetary companion. This culmination of the modeling effort is visualized via the Mass-Distance joint posterior distribution plot.18

### **6.1 Constructing the Mass-Distance Joint Posterior Distribution**

The axes of the Mass-Distance plot represent the final physical coordinates of the lens system. The horizontal x-axis plots the physical distance to the lens (![][image58]), invariably scaled in kiloparsecs (kpc).25 The vertical y-axis plots the mass of the primary lens (![][image57]), strictly scaled in solar masses (![][image60]).25

The Bayesian posterior distribution spanning this plane is visualized using dense, filled contour mapping or solid ellipse boundaries, which rigorously denote the ![][image61], ![][image62], and ![][image63] credible intervals of the parameter inference.25 This plot is the definitive visual statement of the event's physical reality, distilling months of photometric observation and thousands of hours of MCMC computation into a single, highly readable graphic.

### **6.2 Incorporating Galactic Model Priors**

In events where the microlens parallax (![][image64]) or the angular Einstein radius (![][image65]) cannot be measured unambiguously from the lightcurve, the unconstrained Bayesian analysis will produce unacceptably broad probability distributions. To tighten these contours, researchers incorporate strong structural priors derived from Galactic population models.39

A standard Galactic model integrates three key astrophysical distributions: a stellar mass function (such as the Kroupa or Chabrier initial mass functions), a spatial density profile of stars (incorporating a boxy-shaped Galactic bar, an exponential thin disk, and a thick disk), and specific kinematic velocity distributions corresponding to those structural components.39

When visualizing the impact of these priors on the Mass-Distance plane, it is standard practice to plot both the prior distribution (representing the baseline assumptions of the Galactic model) and the final posterior distribution (the prior updated by the specific lightcurve constraints, such as the measured crossing time ![][image66]).35 These are often plotted in adjacent panels, or overlaid as distinct, semi-transparent color maps within the same axis bounds.35 This dual plotting convention visually demonstrates to the reader the restricting power of the observational data over the baseline galactic assumptions.35

### **6.3 Resolving Bimodal Solutions and Empirical Cross-Referencing**

In events plagued by an unresolved close/wide degeneracy, the Mass-Distance plane will clearly display two separated probability islands.14 In such cases, standard plotting protocols require researchers to present the contoured parameters for both potential physical realities—for example, explicitly labeling one probability island as the "Close Model: K-dwarf host" and the other as the "Wide Model: M-dwarf host".14

Furthermore, to benchmark the accuracy of new Bayesian modeling frameworks (such as the pyLIMASS pipeline), researchers will often superimpose the physical parameters derived from legacy, high-resolution empirical follow-ups onto the simulated posterior contours.25 These published benchmark parameters (such as the known properties of the famous event OGLE-2005-BLG-169Lb) are marked on the Mass-Distance plot using explicit geometric symbols, such as large red or black crosses.25 If the central mass of the simulated posterior contours aligns tightly with the cross representing the empirical data, the visualization serves as robust proof of the model's accuracy.25

## **7\. Source Star Characterization: Color-Magnitude Diagrams and Dereddening**

To physically interpret the finite-source effect parameter (![][image67]) and calculate the angular Einstein radius (![][image65]), researchers must accurately determine the intrinsic angular radius of the source star (![][image68]).17 Because microlensing source stars predominantly reside in the highly obscured Galactic Bulge, their apparent observed color and magnitude are heavily corrupted by interstellar dust and extinction.17 Correcting for this obscuration requires the construction and analysis of an instrument-calibrated Color-Magnitude Diagram (CMD).17

### **7.1 Populating the Instrumental Color-Magnitude Diagram**

The axes of the microlensing CMD are defined by the specific photometric filters utilized during the observation. The horizontal x-axis plots the color index, typically defined as the difference between two bands, such as ![][image69] or ![][image70].17 The vertical y-axis plots the apparent magnitude in the primary, most densely sampled monitoring band, typically the ![][image71]\-band or ![][image72]\-band.17

To establish the background stellar environment, the CMD is populated by querying survey catalogs (e.g., OGLE-III or KMTNet) for all field stars located within a small, specific radius centered exactly on the microlensing event coordinates.17 A standard radius for this query is 2 arcminutes or a 120-arcsecond square.17 These thousands of background stars are plotted as small, faint points (often formatted as small black dots, gray scatter, or a 2D density histogram) to visually outline the structure of the Main Sequence and the Giant Branch of the local Galactic population.44

### **7.2 Red Clump Centroid Calibration and Extinction Measurement**

The critical feature within this background population is the Red Clump (RC). Red Clump giants possess a relatively uniform absolute luminosity and intrinsic color, allowing them to act as standard candles within the Galactic Bulge.17 Following the standard methodology established by Nataf et al. (2013), the geometric centroid of the Red Clump is calculated from the background scatter.17 This centroid is plotted on the CMD as a highly visible, oversized marker, typically a large red star or an open circle.44

Simultaneously, the apparent color and magnitude of the microlensed source star must be plotted. Because the source star is often blended with the lens or ambient stars at baseline, its unblended apparent color (![][image73]) and magnitude (![][image74]) are derived from a regression of the multi-band flux observed specifically during the magnification event, combined with the blend parameters extracted from the lightcurve modeling.17 This apparent position of the source star is plotted on the CMD using a distinct, contrasting marker, such as a solid blue dot.17

### **7.3 Deriving the Intrinsic Source Radius**

Because the Red Clump acts as a standard candle, the geometric offset vector on the CMD between the observed position of the RC centroid and the known, intrinsic, extinction-free position of the RC provides a direct, localized measurement of the line-of-sight extinction (![][image75]) and reddening (![][image76]).17

This calibration vector is effectively visualized as a shift applied to the source star. By applying the measured extinction and reddening to the apparent source coordinates, the source star is shifted to its intrinsic, dereddened coordinates ![][image77] and ![][image78].17 Once the intrinsic color and magnitude are visually established on the dereddened CMD, standard empirical surface brightness relations are utilized to calculate the angular radius of the source star (![][image68]). Combined with the normalized source size ![][image67] derived from the lightcurve, the angular Einstein radius is definitively calculated as ![][image79], securing a foundational physical constraint for the event.17

## **8\. Astrometric Microlensing: Luminous Centroid Shifts and Trajectory Mapping**

While photometric microlensing relies on detecting the temporal change in the total integrated flux of the unresolved images, astrometric microlensing tracks the spatial displacement of the luminous centroid of those combined images across the sky.1 Because the physical size of the Einstein ring scales directly with the square root of the lens mass (![][image80]), astrometry offers an incredibly powerful, direct probe of the lens mass that avoids many of the degeneracies inherent in pure photometry.3

### **8.1 The Scale and Physics of Astrometric Shifts**

Plotting astrometric microlensing requires extreme precision. The typical value for an astrometric centroid shift generated by a Galactic Bulge event—for instance, a ![][image81] lens located halfway between the observer and the source—is on the order of ![][image82] to ![][image83] milliarcseconds (mas).4 High-precision space observatories are required to measure these minuscule deviations. While Gaia achieves an uncertainty of approximately ![][image84] mas per epoch (which requires aggressive modeling to extract signals), the Roman Space Telescope's Galactic Bulge Time-Domain Survey is expected to achieve ![][image85] mas uncertainty on individual measurements, detecting astrometric shifts as low as ![][image86]as.4

### **8.2 Plotting the Lensed vs. Unlensed Centroid Trajectory**

Astrometric plots are fundamentally maps of the sky, tracing the 2D path of the image centroid over time. The plotting aesthetics for these trajectories follow strict conventions to ensure clarity.19

The unlensed proper motion of the background source star—the straight path it would have taken if the lens were not present—is plotted as a smooth background trajectory, frequently rendered as a solid red line.19 Crucially, this line represents the physical position of the source, not the centroid of the lensed images.19 The microlensed centroid trajectory is plotted over this baseline. During the peak of the magnification event, the centroid deviates from the unlensed path, creating a distinct elliptical, looping, or arc-like trace before returning to the baseline trajectory.19

The position and unlensed proper motion of the foreground lens star must also be visualized. This is typically indicated by a dashed black line, with distinct red and black arrows placed at the origin of the trajectories to designate the direction and magnitude of the proper motion vectors for the source and the lens, respectively.19 To provide a temporal context connecting the astrometric loop to the photometric lightcurve, the unlensed trajectory line is annotated with evenly spaced dots representing set chronological intervals (e.g., dots spaced at 100-day intervals).19

### **8.3 The Impact of Blending on Astrometric Visualization**

A critical complication in plotting astrometric trajectories is the impact of luminous blending.4 In many events, the foreground lens is a luminous star rather than a dark remnant. Therefore, the actual measured centroid tracked by the telescope is the flux-weighted average of the lensed source images *and* the intrinsic luminosity of the lens itself.4

Astrometric plots must mathematically account for the blend ratio, defined as ![][image87] (the ratio of lens flux to source flux).4 When the lens is luminous, the unlensed reference point for the centroid shift is no longer the physical position of the source star, but rather the blended center of light.47 The presence of non-lensed ambient light fundamentally decreases the amplitude of the observed astrometric excursion, pulling the loop tighter toward the blended center of light. Software generating astrometric plots must include the blend ratio ![][image88] as a foundational parameter when calculating the true shape and scale of the centroid loop.3

## **9\. Kinematic Distributions and Proper Motion Vector Maps**

The relative proper motion between the lens and the source (![][image89]) dictates both the temporal timescale and the geometric likelihood of a microlensing event.17 Beyond timing, the kinematics of the event serve as a critical diagnostic tool for classifying the stellar population of the lens. Visualizing the proper motion vectors allows researchers to definitively differentiate between a lens residing in the Galactic disk and a lens residing in the Galactic bulge.40

### **9.1 Visualizing Relative Proper Motion**

Kinematic properties are visualized using 2D proper motion vector maps. These plots display the proper motion velocities—typically in units of mas/yr—across polar coordinates, Cartesian Right Ascension and Declination planes, or Galactic Longitude and Latitude grids.19 The relative proper motion of the event is plotted as a specific coordinate point or vector arrow on this grid.50

Because kinematic measurements derived from Gaia or high-resolution adaptive optics follow-ups contain inherent uncertainties in both axes, the proper motion vector is never plotted as a simple point. It must be bounded by error ellipses.50 These ellipses rigorously account for the standard errors in both spatial coordinates, as well as the statistical correlation coefficients between them, providing a true geometric representation of the velocity uncertainty.50

### **9.2 Directional Assumptions and Population Grouping**

In many photometric microlensing events, the analysis of the microlens parallax (![][image64]) yields a highly constrained magnitude for the vector, but suffers from a severe degeneracy regarding the vector's angle (direction).50 When visualizing the kinematics of such an event, plotting guidelines require the display of multiple potential proper motion vectors representing the degenerate solutions. This is often achieved by assuming cardinal directions for the parallax vector (e.g., North, East, South, West) and plotting the resulting proper motion coordinates in distinct, highly contrasting colors (e.g., red, green, blue, magenta).50

To draw physical conclusions from these vectors, the plot must include the expected proper motion dispersion profiles for standard Galactic populations. Large, shaded background ellipses (e.g., cyan tracks) are overlaid on the plot to delineate the known velocity dispersion of the Galactic disk and the Galactic bar/bulge.50 If one of the specific proper motion tracks calculated from the event aligns cleanly within the kinematic boundary of the Galactic bar, while the others fall into non-physical or empty velocity spaces, the visualization provides overwhelming Bayesian support for the conclusion that the lens is a member of the bulge population.50

## **10\. Galactic Context: Optical Depth, 3D Dust Maps, and Terminal Velocity Plots**

Because microlensing relies entirely on the precise, chance spatial alignment of two independent stars, the fundamental frequency and distribution of events are dictated by the structural mass and kinematic velocity distribution of the Milky Way galaxy itself.15 This alignment probability is quantified by the concept of optical depth (![][image90]), which represents the fraction of the sky covered by the Einstein rings of all potential lenses, or conversely, the instantaneous probability that any given source star is undergoing a microlensing event.53 Visualizing optical depth and Galactic structure is vital for survey planning and testing competing models of the Milky Way.

### **10.1 Integrating and Mapping Microlensing Optical Depth**

The optical depth is fundamentally a line-of-sight integration through the mass density of the galaxy. It is mathematically defined as:

![][image91]  
where ![][image92] represents the spatial distribution of mass (including stars, remnants, and dark matter) along the path to the source.55

Optical depth maps are plotted as wide-field spatial maps across Galactic coordinates, with Galactic Longitude (![][image93]) on the horizontal x-axis and Galactic Latitude (![][image94]) on the vertical y-axis.56 Because the probability of microlensing is extremely low, the raw numerical values of optical depth are universally scaled to units of ![][image95] for plotting purposes (e.g., a measured optical depth of ![][image96] is plotted simply as ![][image97], and denoted as ![][image98]).52

The spatial variation of ![][image90] across the Galactic Bulge is visualized via contour lines. Standard mapping convention dictates using varying line styles (e.g., solid, dashed, and dot-dashed lines) to indicate specific optical depth boundaries, such as ![][image99], ![][image100], and ![][image85].56 These contours are typically overlaid on grayscale or continuous color heatmaps that represent the underlying event density or stellar density.56 To validate theoretical models against empirical data, observational estimates from major surveys (such as the 8000 events cataloged by OGLE-IV, or data from MACHO and MOA) are plotted as specific data points or localized boxes over the theoretical background predicted by structural frameworks like the Besançon Galaxy Model.15

### **10.2 High-Resolution 3D Dust and Extinction Maps**

The visual observation of the Galactic center is severely hampered by dense clouds of interstellar dust. To calculate accurate mass-luminosity relations and interpret CMDs, researchers must integrate high-resolution 3D dust maps into their visualization pipelines.43

Standard plotting guidelines for Galactic dust maps—such as those calibrated against the Two-Micron All-Sky Survey (2MASS)—represent extinction over fine spatial resolutions, typically 15 arcminutes.58 These wide-field maps display the Galactic plane with intensive, high-contrast color scaling (e.g., deep purples or dark reds) tracing the highest extinction zones near ![][image101], clearly defining the areas where optical surveys will fail and near-infrared surveys (like Roman) are required.56 Advanced population synthesis codes, such as PopSyCLE, utilize these integrated dust maps to simulate realistic photometric observations of microlensing events, plotting the spatial distribution of expected events against the obscuration of the dust.16

### **10.3 Terminal Velocity Plots and Mass-Density Calibration**

To calculate accurate optical depths, the theoretical mass-to-light ratios (![][image102]) of the Galactic models must be properly calibrated. This calibration is visualized using terminal velocity plots.52

Terminal velocity plots are longitude-velocity (![][image103]) diagrams designed to compare the kinematics of interstellar gas in the inner Galaxy with the gravitational potential generated by model mass distributions.52 The x-axis plots the Galactic Longitude (![][image93] in degrees), and the y-axis plots the velocity (![][image104] in km/s).52 Observational terminal velocity measurements derived from radio frequency emission-line studies (such as CO and HI gas kinematics) are plotted as distinct geometric markers (e.g., diamonds, squares, or stars).52 The theoretical predictions derived from Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH) gas flow models are plotted over the data as continuous curves of varying thickness, representing different structural assumptions (e.g., varying the inversion angle of the Galactic bar).52 The alignment of the SPH curves with the observational data points visually validates the mass-density profile of the Galactic model, which in turn solidifies the theoretical optical depth calculations.52

| Galactic Visualization | Axes and Coordinates | Standard Units / Scaling | Analytical Purpose |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Optical Depth (![][image90]) Map** | Galactic Longitude (![][image93]) vs. Latitude (![][image94]) | Scaled to ![][image95] (![][image98]) | Uses contour lines to map the spatial probability of microlensing alignments across the Bulge. |
| **3D Dust / Extinction Map** | Galactic Longitude (![][image93]) vs. Latitude (![][image94]) | Magnitudes / 15-arcmin resolution | Uses intense color gradients to highlight regions of severe optical obscuration, guiding infrared survey pointing. |
| **Terminal Velocity (![][image103]) Plot** | Longitude (![][image93]) vs. Velocity (![][image104]) | Degrees vs. km/s | Compares observed gas kinematics to theoretical mass-density models, validating the gravitational potential of the Galaxy. |

## **11\. Software Toolkits and Standardized Implementations for Visualization**

Given the extreme mathematical complexity involved in generating finite-source lightcurves, integrating caustic crossings, and mapping high-dimensional Bayesian posteriors, the microlensing community has largely moved away from bespoke scripts in favor of standardized, open-source, object-oriented modeling software. The two preeminent software packages dictating modern visualization and plotting standards are pyLIMA (Python Lightcurve Identification and Microlensing Analysis) and MulensModel.7

### **11.1 Automated Visualization Workflows in pyLIMA**

pyLIMA was developed explicitly to handle the massive data deluge expected from the Roman Space Telescope's Galactic Bulge Time Domain Survey. It provides an automated, end-to-end framework capable of ingesting raw time-series photometry and generating publication-ready standard visualizations.7

pyLIMA includes a suite of diverse fitting algorithms designed to navigate the chaotic parameter space: Levenberg-Marquardt algorithms for rapid initial fits, Differential Evolution for robust exploration of the highly non-linear parameter boundaries, and MCMC samplers for ultimate posterior corner-plot generation.26 When a user initializes a pyLIMA event object, the framework can automatically execute a full plotting routine that simultaneously generates the primary photometric lightcurve overlaid on the data, the corresponding residuals panel aligned chronologically below it, and a coordinated geometric plot displaying the lens plane, caustic structures, and source trajectory.64

Furthermore, pyLIMA features advanced operational modules such as RTModel (Real-Time Model). RTModel is a sophisticated, automated microlensing package that performs hands-off heuristic grid-search mapping.26 It autonomously executes a massive grid search across a template library, determines the correct topological model type (single-lens, binary-lens, or binary-source), and outputs the resulting ![][image105] likelihood maps without requiring manual intervention, standardizing the search for global minima across the community.26

### **11.2 Object-Oriented Plotting Architectures in MulensModel**

While pyLIMA focuses heavily on automated pipelines, MulensModel provides researchers with highly granular, object-oriented control over the modeling and visualization process.13 Built entirely in Python 3, MulensModel leverages the widespread matplotlib library under the hood, ensuring that all generated plots meet the stringent aesthetic requirements of professional astronomical journals.61

The architectural workflow in MulensModel is distinctly object-oriented. The user first defines a Model object by passing a dictionary of specific parameters—for instance, defining ![][image106] and ![][image66] for a PSPL model, or ![][image107] and ![][image108] for a 2-body binary lens.12 Observational datasets are subsequently imported into isolated MulensData objects, which handle the specific temporal cadences and baseline flux offsets of different telescopes.13 Finally, the model and the datasets are combined into a master Event object.13

Visualization is executed through explicit, highly configurable class methods. To plot the theoretical magnification curve in isolation, the user calls plot\_magnification(), passing a specific temporal t\_range.13 To streamline axes formatting, the boolean parameter subtract\_2450000=True is natively built into the plotting methods to automatically standardize the HJD time axis.13 To visualize the goodness-of-fit, the user calls event.plot\_model() to overlay the model line, and event.plot\_data() to plot the observatory scatter points.13 Crucially, MulensModel automatically handles the complex task of flux scaling during this step, adjusting the baseline flux of the theoretical model to match the primary dataset.13 In scenarios where the source crosses a caustic and finite-source effects are required, the user invokes the set\_magnification\_methods() command, which deploys sophisticated adaptive contouring algorithms (such as the 'VBBL' method) to accurately calculate and plot the smoothed magnification curve across the divergent boundaries, preventing visual artifacts in the final lightcurve.12

## **Conclusion**

The visual grammar of gravitational microlensing is not merely a downstream tool for data presentation; it is a foundational, active mechanism for physical discovery. From the subtle, asymmetric parallax wiggles plotted in the binned residuals of a photometric lightcurve, to the topological symmetry defining the close/wide degeneracy on a ![][image105] grid search map, these standardized plotting conventions allow researchers to navigate one of the most mathematically complex and highly degenerate parameter spaces in modern astrophysics. By rigidly adhering to the comprehensive guidelines outlined in this playbook—whether tracking centroid shifts on an astrometric trajectory map, deriving intrinsic source radii via dereddened Color-Magnitude Diagrams, or mapping the intersection of lens fluxes on a mass-distance banana plot—investigators can systematically decode the physical reality of the Galactic Bulge. As the community transitions into the era of the Roman Space Telescope, the strict application of these visualization standards will be the mechanism that translates raw, chaotic photometric arrays into the next generation of exoplanetary demographics.
